dependencies: Natural gas supplies from western Canada, Rocky Mountains, and southwestern United States
Electricity procurement from third-party power purchase agreements
Interstate pipeline transportation from third-party providers
Dependence on contractors for vegetation management, asset inspections, and construction work
risks: Natural gas transportation and storage dependencies
Supply chain disruptions from catastrophic events
Longer lead times and delivery delays for equipment, materials, and supplies
Single largest natural gas supplier represented approximately 50% of total natural gas volume purchased in 2024
Concentration risk in nuclear fuel supply chain
Supply chain disruptions due to domestic and international raw material and labor shortages
Inflation and tariffs increasing costs for equipment, materials, contractor services
Contractor performance risks - less control over contractors but retain liability for quality and completion
Dependence on third-party power producers for renewable energy compliance

regulatory_risks: OEIS may determine Utility failed to substantially comply with Wildfire Mitigation Plan, resulting in loss of safety certification
FERC Section 203 approval required for mergers, consolidations, change of control transactions
California GHG emissions targets: 40% reduction by 2030, carbon neutrality by 2045, 100% renewable/zero-carbon electricity by 2045
FERC has authority to impose fines up to $1 million per day for violations
Mandatory electric reliability standards enforced by FERC to protect bulk power system
Extensive federal, state, and local energy, environmental, and safety regulations subject to enforcement proceedings
CPUC may not authorize sufficient revenues or may disallow costs in ratemaking proceedings
Ownership restrictions in Amended Articles limiting shareholders to 4.75% combined value of equity securities
California privacy laws including California Privacy Rights Act with final regulations under development
FERC rate case outcomes and transmission cost recovery
Cargo Preference Act compliance for DOE loan
SB 100 requirement for 60% renewables by 2030 and 100% renewable/carbon-free by 2045 with potential penalties for non-compliance
Wildfire Fund reimbursement obligations if found imprudent
Loss of safety certification would eliminate access to Wildfire Fund and presumption of prudency under AB 1054
CPUC conditions governing relationship between Utility and PG&E Corporation including capital structure requirements
Pipeline and Hazardous Materials Safety Administration regulations for natural gas facilities
Environmental compliance costs and remediation liabilities
CPUC Safety and Enforcement Division can impose penalties up to $100,000 per day per violation, with administrative limit of $8 million per citation
NRC regulations may require substantial costs for nuclear facility compliance or force shutdown of DCPP
AB 1054 safety certification requirements and potential loss
California Public Utilities Code Section 451.1 prudency standard
Potential changes to federal environmental policies under Trump administration creating regulatory uncertainty
Nuclear Waste Policy Act obligations for spent fuel disposal
California Geologic Energy Management Division regulations for underground gas storage
DOE Loan Guarantee Agreement compliance requirements and conditions for advances
CPUC reasonableness review may result in cost disallowances for wildfire mitigation expenditures
Environmental regulations under EPA, CARB, and state agencies including Resource Conservation and Recovery Act, Comprehensive Environmental Response Compensation and Liability Act
Davis-Bacon Act compliance requirements for DOE loan
National Environmental Policy Act review requirements for DOE loan projects
CPUC Enhanced Oversight and Enforcement Process could result in appointment of independent monitor, chief restructuring officer, receivership, or review of operating license
Section 382 IRC ownership change limitations on use of net operating loss carryforwards
Nuclear Regulatory Commission licensing and operational requirements
Rate regulation changes affecting cost recovery mechanisms
CARB cap-and-trade program with declining emissions caps through 2030 and potential reforms to reduce allowance budgets
Permits, authorizations, and licenses required from multiple federal, state, and local agencies for facility construction and operation
CPUC cost recovery disallowances for wildfire-related costs
Potential penalties for non-compliance with wildfire mitigation requirements including EPSS, PSPS, vegetation management, asset inspections
Franchise agreements with approximately 300 cities and counties for use of public streets and highways
CPUC holding company rules and capital structure requirements
CPUC authorization required for securities issuances, asset dispositions, and major transactions
Potential municipalization attempts through eminent domain by jurisdictions including San Francisco
Climate change and greenhouse gas emission regulations
CPUC prudency review of capital expenditures and operating costs
New regulations on wildfire mitigation and prevention
Water quality and environmental remediation regulations
Potential penalties and fines from regulatory non-compliance
